title: Vikas Mishra
text:
Hello, My name is Vikas Mishra. I am a chip designer by profession and have been doing this for about 25 years. I have worked for companies such as Texas Instruments, Nvidia, Qualcomm, Intel and Google. I have worked on many key chips that have hopefully made the world a sligthly better and a more comfortable place. If you have ever used a DSL modem, or an Android tablet, or a laptop, chances that I have worked on it.
I am trying to learn the new field of Deep Learning. And I am hoping this blog, will help me in documenting my journey.

title: Quarto Basics and Prime Numbers
text:
For a demonstration of a line plot on a polar axis, see Figure 1.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

r = np.arange(0, 2, 0.01)
theta = 2 * np.pi * r
fig, ax = plt.subplots(
  subplot_kw = {'projection': 'polar'} 
)
ax.plot(theta, r)
ax.set_rticks([0.5, 1, 1.5, 2])
ax.grid(True)
plt.show()




Figure 1: A line plot on a polar axis




Let’s see how well it handles a python code that I wrote (or rather asked chatGPT to write) for finding out a prime number between 1 to N. N is a number input by the user

# Define a function to check if a number is prime or not
def is_prime(n):
    # If n is less than 2, it is not prime
    if n < 2:
        return False
    # Loop from 2 to the square root of n
    for i in range(2, int(n**0.5) + 1):
        # If n is divisible by i, it is not prime
        if n % i == 0:
            return False
    # If none of the above conditions are met, n is prime
    return True

# Define a function to print all prime numbers between 1 and N
def print_primes(N):
    # Loop from 1 to N
    for i in range(1, N + 1):
        # If i is prime, print it
        if is_prime(i):
            print(i)

# Get the input from the user
N = int(input("Enter a positive integer: "))

# Call the function with the input
print_primes(N)

Enter a positive integer:  135
